summary:
Philip II of Macedon (r. 359–336 BC) was the king who transformed Macedonia from a vulnerable frontier kingdom into the hegemon of Greece, creating the military machine and political structures that made possible the world-conquering campaigns of his son Alexander. Philip is sometimes overlooked in the shadow of Alexander's legend, but many historians regard him as the more subtle and accomplished statesman.

Philip came to power at a moment of crisis, with Macedonia facing simultaneous attacks from multiple neighbors. He dealt with each sequentially through a combination of force, diplomacy, and marriage alliances. He had spent time as a hostage in Thebes during the [[event:1021|Theban Hegemony]], where he observed Epaminondas's revolutionary tactics — the concentrated assault, the oblique order — and adapted them to Macedonian conditions. His reformed phalanx, armed with the sarissa (an 18-foot pike), combined with the shock cavalry of the Companions, was the most formidable military system the Greek world had produced.

Philip's interference in the Sacred Wars, his conquest of Thessaly, and his eventual dominance of Greek politics produced fierce opposition led by the Athenian orator Demosthenes, whose Philippic speeches became a byword for denunciatory rhetoric. The [[event:1029|Battle of Chaeronea]] in 338 BC was Philip's strategic masterpiece, in which he commanded the right wing with the teenage Alexander commanding the cavalry on the left, destroying the Greek coalition including the [[event:1022|Sacred Band of Thebes]]. Philip was assassinated in 336 BC at his daughter's wedding, possibly with the connivance of his wife Olympias, leaving Alexander to inherit an instrument ready for world conquest.
